FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; secondary analysis of adolescent and parent data collected from baseline through 3-year follow-up in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures problematic social media use (PSMU) via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale; item example: 'I feel the need to use social media sites/apps more and more'

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects linear modeling examining association between cumulative ACEs score at baseline and PSMU at follow-up; reports coefficient for ACEs → PSMU association

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β=1.5, p<0.001 for cumulative ACEs (≥4) versus low ACEs (<2) predicting higher PSMU

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects linear modeling with secondary data from ABCD baseline through 3-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Issues in Mental Health Nursing, 2025; DOI: 10.1080/01612840.2025.2559062

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological predictor (ACEs exposure). E16 applies: individual-characteristic predictors of PSMU are MET regardless of direction/timeframe. Not demographic-only predictor.
notes: Emotional and behavioral dysregulation did not moderate the ACEs-PSMU association. Diverse sample of young US adolescents (10-14 years).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (data release 5.0); longitudinal cohort comprising 11,875 children born 2005-2009, enrolled at ages 9-10 years from 21 US sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures social media use (estimated hours/day on social media) via Youth Screen Time Survey; also measures video games and television/videos separately

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models with random intercept and slopes testing social media use × time interaction on ADHD-related symptoms; two-way and three-way interactions tested

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports social media × time: β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001 for inattention symptoms; cumulative 4-year effect β = 0.15 (SE, 0.03; P < .001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; 4-year follow-up design with mixed-effects modeling examining change in ADHD symptoms over time

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Pediatrics Open Science, 2026; peer-reviewed original research

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual genetic predictor (polygenic risk score for ADHD [PGS-ADHD]). E16 applies: individual-characteristic predictors of phone/SM are MET regardless of direction/timeframe.
notes: Cross-lagged panel models confirmed unidirectional association: social media use predicted inattention symptoms (β=0.03, P=.004), but inattention did not predict increased social media use (β=-0.002, P=.64).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (release 5.1, collected September 2016–January 2022); diverse sample of 1,940 participants aged 11-12 years at Wave 1

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures social media addiction (SMA) scores via 6-item questionnaire adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale; also measures time spent on social media (SMT)

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression reporting associations between SMA and binge eating (BE) symptoms at cross-sectional (Wave 1, 3) and prospective (Wave 1 to Wave 3) timepoints

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports addiction SMA group: PR 2.17 (95% CI: 1.22–3.84) at Wave 1, PR 3.45 (95% CI: 1.83–6.49) at Wave 3; prospective RR 1.90 (95% CI: 1.10–3.27); trajectory models: consistently high SMA RR 3.01 (95% CI: 2.07–4.37)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; 3-wave design with latent class trajectory modeling and modified Poisson regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Obesity (Silver Spring), 2025; DOI: 10.1002/oby.24199

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/behavioral predictor (binge eating behavior). Also reports cross-sectional analyses with SMA as IV. Also reports moderation analyses by sex and BMI. Multiple reasons FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: SMA scores, but not SMT alone, were prospectively associated with BE. Associations stronger in male adolescents and varied by baseline BMI. Latent class trajectory analysis identified four distinct SMA trajectory groups.
